name: Potassium
description: Potassium is the major intracellular cation responsible for maintaining the resting membrane potential of cells, particularly critical for cardiac and skeletal muscle function.
clinical_use: Used to evaluate electrolyte balance, cardiac arrhythmias, muscle weakness, renal function, acid-base status, and to monitor diuretic or anti-hypertensive therapies.

references.source: Mayo Clinic Laboratories, Pediatric Reference Values
Mayo Clinic Laboratories, Pediatric Reference Values
Mayo Clinic Laboratories, Pediatric Reference Values
Clinical and Laboratory Standards Institute (CLSI) Guidelines

interpretation_rules.meaning: Hypokalemia (Adult threshold; pediatric thresholds vary by age)
Hyperkalemia (Adult threshold; neonates and infants have naturally higher limits)

critical_values.meaning: Severe hypokalemia, increased risk of cardiac arrhythmias and muscle paralysis
Severe hyperkalemia, extreme risk of ventricular fibrillation and cardiac arrest

warnings: In vitro hemolysis is a frequent cause of falsely elevated serum potassium (pseudohyperkalemia).
Prolonged tourniquet use or fist clenching during venipuncture can artificially increase potassium levels.
Heel stick sampling in neonates may cause artifactual elevation due to hemolysis or tissue fluid contamination.
notes: Serum potassium levels are tightly linked to acid-base balance; acidosis drives potassium out of cells, while alkalosis drives it into cells.
Neonatal and infant upper reference limits are naturally higher than adults due to immature renal function and physiological differences. Interpretation of hyperkalemia must be strictly age-adjusted.

references.title: Physiology and pathophysiology of potassium homeostasis
